summary: Joel responds to a reader asking whether companies should pay for interview travel expenses. Rather than directly answering, he advises the candidate to batch multiple interviews into a single trip to the UK, minimizing personal cost while maximizing opportunities. He then explains how mentioning competing employers triggers psychological principles of social proof and scarcity, making the candidate appear more desirable and potentially motivating companies to cover travel costs.
key_points: Batch multiple interviews into one trip to spread costs and increase your odds of landing a job
Mentioning other potential employers triggers social proof, making you seem more desirable as a candidate
The principle of scarcity means employers will worry about losing you to competitors, driving up your market value
The strategic framing of your situation matters more than directly asking for expense reimbursement
money_quotes: I'd be happy to interview in person. I'm planning a trip to the UK for all the interviews in two months. See you then.
Worst case scenario, you don't spend very much money on the trip, you almost certainly get a job, and you probably even have your choice of employers.
There's a good chance the mere mention of other potential employers will make these guys realize you're actually in heavy demand.
When they think that other employers are competing for your services, you'll seem like a more desirable candidate. This is the principle of social proof.
Opportunities seem more valuable to us when their availability is limited.
The combination will drive up your market value, and probably even motivate the hiring company to pay for the flight.
key_insight: Rather than asking employers to pay for interview travel, candidates gain more leverage by batching interviews and letting the implied competition between employers do the negotiating for them.
